findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the NEW `_load_acli()` return-value contract explicitly in the Approach section — e.g. 'returns a `TicketTransport` directly' or 'returns a `Backend`; callers change to `_load_acli().transport`' — and state what shape the 60+ test doubles must become (transport-shaped `SimpleNamespace(search_issues=..., create_issue=...)` or backend-shaped) so implementers can update them uniformly.
evidence: Plan: 'evolve their existing _load_acli seams to yield the backend transport and update their patch.object tests in the same change'
Plan: '23 test files use that seam — they inject types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client); the seam keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy'
Code: applier.py:443 `client = acli_mod.AcliClient(jira_url=_s.url, user=_s.user, api_token=_s.api_token)` — callers destructure the module return value
Code: 60+ test sites inject module-shaped doubles (types.SimpleNamespace with AcliClient attribute or types.ModuleType('acli_integration') with AcliClient set), confirmed by grep across tests/

ABSENCE: The plan does not specify what `_load_acli()` returns post-change (a Backend? a TicketTransport? a wrapped namespace?) nor what the new call-site shape is at applier.py:443 and fetcher.py:371
findings.finding: The plan says to 'evolve the _load_acli seam to yield the backend transport' but never specifies the new return-value CONTRACT that callers and the 60+ existing test doubles must conform to after the change. Currently _load_acli() returns an acli MODULE and callers do `acli_mod.AcliClient(...)`. If the seam now returns a TicketTransport object, callers must change to use it directly; if it returns a Backend, callers must do `_load_acli().transport`. The plan says 'the seam keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy' — circular phrasing that doesn't tell an implementer what shape to produce or what shape the 60+ `SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)` doubles must become. Without this specification, an implementer who picks the wrong shape silently breaks all test doubles without the neutrality gate catching it (the gate checks imports, not seam shapes).
findings.location: Approach section — '_load_acli seam' paragraph; AC bullet 4 ('The existing applier/fetcher _load_acli seams and their 23 patch.object tests pass with the seam now yielding the backend transport')
scenarios: Implementer makes _load_acli return a TicketTransport; callers at applier.py:443 still do `acli_mod.AcliClient(...)` → AttributeError at runtime, not caught by neutrality gate
Implementer makes _load_acli return a Backend and changes callers to `.transport`, but leaves all 60+ test doubles as module-shaped → every patched test fails with AttributeError on `.transport`
The test-double migration scope is ambiguous: 'update their patch.object tests in lockstep' doesn't say whether doubles must become transport-shaped or backend-shaped
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach paragraph: 'Post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the `TicketTransport` directly; call sites change from `acli_mod.AcliClient(jira_url=..., ...)` to `_load_acli()` (no further construction); test doubles become `SimpleNamespace(search_issues=lambda **_: ..., create_issue=lambda **_: ..., ...)` matching the `TicketTransport` protocol surface.' Alternatively, if returning a Backend is preferred, specify that shape instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-count the test files that `patch.object` the `_load_acli` seam (grep finds ~34 distinct files, not 23) and update the Approach section count so the executor knows the full migration surface.
evidence: Grep of tests/ for '_load_acli' returned hits across: test_transition_error_soft_fail, test_applier_comment_error_surfaced, test_applier_rebase_retry, test_applier, test_applier_mapping, test_applier_provenance, test_applier_drift, test_applier_stale_binding_404, test_applier_assignee_soft_fail, test_silent_noop_canary, test_applier_reschedule, test_applier_cross_project_guard, test_applier_create_comment_error_surfaced, test_fail_loud_comment_errors, test_parent_sync, test_status_transition_rest, test_conflict_filing_deferred, test_bridge_alerts_surface, test_fetcher_snapshot, test_fetcher_dedup_observable, test_fetcher_truncation_gate, test_fetcher_split_jql_queries, test_fetcher_project_scoped_jql, test_fetcher_pagination_jql, test_fetcher_unmapped_status, test_assignee_validation, test_comment_enrichment, test_link_sync, test_hard_delete_recreate, test_inbound_leaf_bodies, test_apply_batch_drift_tolerance, test_e2e_dedup_pass, test_reconcile_once, test_reconcile_idempotency — totaling ~34 distinct files plus integration/_fakes.py.
Plan says '23 test files use that seam — they inject types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)'
findings.finding: The plan states '23 test files use that seam' (the _load_acli patch.object seam across applier and fetcher), but a grep of the test tree finds approximately 34 distinct test files patching _load_acli. This empirically wrong count could cause the executor to underestimate the migration surface and miss files when updating the seam contract.
findings.location: Approach section: 'evolve the seam + its patch.object tests in the same change (23 test files use that seam'
scenarios: Executor updates 23 files and considers the work complete, leaving ~11 files still patching the old module-returning seam, causing test failures or stale patches that silently pass because the old seam still exists.
The AC verification step ('all verified by make test') catches the failures but without a corrected count the executor doesn't know where to look.
findings.suggested_fix: Run `grep -rl '_load_acli' tests/ | wc -l` and update the count in the Approach section; also enumerate any that use `monkeypatch.setattr` instead of `patch.object` so the seam-update pass covers all injection styles.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Recount the test files that patch `_load_acli` on `applier` or `fetcher` (grep confirms 30+, not 23); update the Approach / AC#4 with the accurate count so the executor knows the full blast radius of the seam change.
evidence: Plan: '23 test files use that seam — they inject types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)'

Grep for `_load_acli` in tests finds patches in: test_transition_error_soft_fail, test_applier_comment_error_surfaced, test_applier_rebase_retry, test_applier (8 patches), test_applier_mapping, test_applier_provenance, test_applier_drift, test_applier_stale_binding_404, test_applier_assignee_soft_fail, test_silent_noop_canary, test_applier_reschedule, test_applier_cross_project_guard, test_applier_create_comment_error_surfaced, test_fail_loud_comment_errors, test_parent_sync, test_bridge_alerts_surface, test_fetcher_snapshot, test_fetcher_dedup_observable, test_fetcher_truncation_gate, test_fetcher_split_jql_queries, test_fetcher_project_scoped_jql, test_fetcher_pagination_jql, test_fetcher_unmapped_status, test_conflict_filing_deferred, test_hard_delete_recreate, test_inbound_leaf_bodies, test_apply_batch_drift_tolerance, test_e2e_dedup_pass, test_reconcile_once, test_reconcile_idempotency, jira_contract/_fakes.py, test_comment_enrichment, test_link_sync — 33+ distinct files
findings.finding: The plan states 'the existing 23 test files use that [_load_acli] seam', but a grep across the test suite finds 30+ distinct test files that patch `applier._load_acli` or `fetcher._load_acli`. The undercount is material: the plan uses '23 test files' to scope the lockstep seam update in the Approach section and AC#4; if the true number is higher, the executor may miss patching some test files when the seam contract changes.
findings.location: Approach section — 'evolve their existing _load_acli seams … 23 test files use that seam'; AC#4
scenarios: Executor updates 23 test files and ships; remaining 10+ files still patch the old seam shape and silently pass with the wrong mock (the seam still returns a module-with-AcliClient, just now it's a backend.transport), masking the rewire for those sites.
findings.suggested_fix: Run `grep -r '_load_acli' tests/ | grep -v Binary | grep 'patch\|setattr\|monkeypatch' | sort -u` to get the definitive count and list it explicitly in the plan.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` is 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' — but `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does not exist anywhere in `acli_graph.py`. Grepping the file finds zero matches for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`, `RELATION`, or `Blocks`. The stale comment inside `outbound_links.py` itself calls this the canonical source '(Cycle 1)', but the symbol was evidently removed or never landed there. This means: (a) there is no extant canonical single-sourced copy to point to; (b) the relocate step for this leak site must CREATE a new canonical definition in `adapters/jira/`, not merge a duplicate; (c) the neutrality AC's assertion that 'outbound_links link constants are single-sourced under adapters/jira/' and 'grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits' may behave unexpectedly if the implementer searches for a symbol that doesn't exist in the place the plan describes.
findings.location: Context / Problem — 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace: 'a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK ("keep in sync with acli_graph")' with: 'the only current definition of the relation→Jira-link-type vocabulary (the stale comment claims `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` is the canonical source, but that symbol does not exist in acli_graph.py — the constant in outbound_links.py IS the sole definition). The relocation step must move this definition into adapters/jira/ and have outbound_links.py import from there.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts '23 test files use that seam' (the `_load_acli` patch.object seam) to scope the test-update work, but a grep confirms at least 33 distinct test files patch `_load_acli`, including several integration test files not under `tests/unit/`. The undercount could cause the implementer to miss updating integration tests in the lockstep change.
findings.location: Approach section — '23 test files use that seam — they inject types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '23 test files' with the actual count from a fresh grep and add a note that integration tests in tests/integration/rebar_reconciler/ use a monkeypatch/setattr pattern rather than patch.object and need separate handling.

findings.finding: The plan describes the outbound_differ.py:63 leak site as importing only `_map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields`, but the actual import block (lines 63-69) re-exports five symbols via `# noqa: F401`: `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field`, and `_map_local_to_jira_fields`. Several test files OUTSIDE core access these via `outbound_differ.<name>` (e.g., `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85` asserts `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531/561/698` calls `outbound_differ._diff_fields`, `test_preflight_status_dict.py:145` uses `outbound_differ._map_local_to_jira_fields`). These callers are unenumerated update targets: if the re-exports are removed to satisfy the neutrality gate, these tests break.
findings.location: Context / Problem (leak sites); outbound_differ.py:63-69; test files: test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85, test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531/561/698, test_preflight_status_dict.py:145
findings.suggested_fix: Add these files to the plan's edit-target list; either redirect them to the adapter (e.g., `adapters.jira.jira_fields._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`) or keep shim re-exports in outbound_differ.py that forward from the adapter, deferring full re-export removal to S5.

findings.finding: The plan's description of the outbound_differ.py:63 leak site lists only `_map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields`, but the actual import at line 63 also re-exports `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, and `_extract_jira_field` with `# noqa: F401`. These additional re-exported vendor symbols are not mentioned in the scope, leak-site inventory, or AC, so the neutrality gate (AC #1) and the rewire scope may silently miss them.
findings.location: Context / Problem — 'Confirmed leak sites' / outbound_differ.py:63; AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Add these three names to the leak-site inventory and AC #1's grep pattern, or add a separate AC bullet explicitly permitting the re-exports with a rationale (e.g. 'test-suite backward-compat shim, removed in S5').

findings.finding: The plan's `_load_acli` seam evolution — 'yielding the backend transport' — breaks the `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` exception-class catch site in `apply_handlers.py:181`. Today `ctx.acli` is the module, carrying both the `AcliClient` factory and exception classes (`AssigneeNotFoundError`). A `TicketTransport` instance cannot carry exception classes without explicit protocol-level plumbing that the plan does not specify, creating a silent breakage path the characterization suite may not catch (exception-class catch sites are exercised only by specific error-path tests, not the happy-path reconcile characterization).
findings.location: Approach section / 'transport/construction sites' paragraph; `apply_handlers.py:181` (`except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError`); AC line 4 (`_load_acli seams and their 23 patch.object tests pass`)
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an `exceptions` namespace or a typed `assignee_not_found_error` attribute to `TicketTransport`, or specify that `BatchApplyContext.acli` is kept as the module (separate from the transport) and only the `client` field is sourced from the backend — making the exception-class resolution an explicit concern in the seam contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the characterization suite: name the make target or script that captures the pre-story baseline, runs the post-refactor suite, and diffs the outputs (e.g., `make characterize-baseline` before the story and `make characterize-diff` after), and reference it in AC5.
evidence: 'the full reconcile suite (outbound + inbound passes over fixture stores) produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story main'
No make target, snapshot file path, or diff command is named — 'pre-story main' is a moving reference that must be captured before the story begins.
AC5 only says 'make lint && make typecheck && make test all green' without distinguishing characterization from unit/integration passes.
findings.finding: The characterization suite's proving command and comparison mechanism are left implicit — the plan describes it as 'the full reconcile suite produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story main' but does not specify how the pre-story baseline is captured, stored, or diffed (e.g., a snapshot file, a re-run against a fixture store, a separate make target).
findings.location: Testing section: 'Characterization: the full reconcile suite (outbound + inbound passes over fixture stores) produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story main — the primary behavior-preserving guard.'
scenarios: An executor runs `make test` and all tests pass, but the characterization comparison was never set up — the primary behavior-preserving guard silently does not exist.
The 'pre-story main' baseline is never snapshotted before the refactor begins, making the comparison impossible post-hoc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concretely how `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` replace each of the three `"jira"` literal call sites in `apply_inbound_records.py` (line 70: `ensure_identity_for("jira", ...)`, line 74 implicitly, line 156: `validate_creation_channel("jira")`) — including which object is passed in and which attribute/method provides the vendor string.
evidence: grep for 'IdentityConvention' across entire repo returns zero hits in source files
grep for 'RemoteRef' returns zero hits in source files (only in cached plan-review JSON artifacts)
apply_inbound_records.py:70 calls rebar.ensure_identity_for("jira", account_id, ...) — the 'jira' is a provider-key string passed to an external function
apply_inbound_records.py:156 calls validate_creation_channel("jira") — the 'jira' is a creation-channel literal validated by an external function
S2 ticket (ce4d) defines RemoteRef as RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} dataclass — no mention of IdentityConvention providing a vendor-key string for these call sites
findings.finding: The plan uses `RemoteRef` and `IdentityConvention` (S2 deliverables) to replace the hardcoded `"jira"` literals in `apply_inbound_records.py`. Neither type exists anywhere in the current codebase. The S2 ticket (ce4d) defines `RemoteRef` but does not mention `IdentityConvention` as an argument to `rebar.ensure_identity_for(...)` or `validate_creation_channel(...)`. The actual code at `apply_inbound_records.py:70/74` calls `rebar.ensure_identity_for("jira", ...)` and at `:156` calls `validate_creation_channel("jira")` — replacing these with `IdentityConvention` requires that `IdentityConvention` exposes the right vendor-key string at those exact call sites, which is not specified.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Replace the apply_inbound_records.py "jira" literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention'; AC line 3: 'apply_inbound_records.py uses RemoteRef/IdentityConvention with no hardcoded "jira" provider/creation-channel literal'
scenarios: Executor attempts to replace ensure_identity_for("jira", ...) with IdentityConvention but the Protocol doesn't expose a vendor-key string compatible with that call signature.
Executor replaces validate_creation_channel("jira") with IdentityConvention.vendor_key or similar, but that attribute name was never specified, causing an AttributeError.

The replacement is semantically wrong: IdentityConvention.vendor == 'jira' only for JiraBackend, but the apply_inbound_records code is called after backend selection — so the right value could be retrieved from the backend, but that requires a different code path than suggested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `run_differs` new seam uses the same `sys.modules` pre-registration injection pattern as `_load()` (not a direct `select_backend(load_config())` call), so tests can pre-register a fake transport under a known key without triggering `load_config()`; and confirm the new seam is compatible with standalone `spec_from_file_location` test loading.
evidence: Code: run_differs.py uses `from rebar_reconciler import acli as acli_mod_for_comments` DEFERRED inside function body specifically to 'avoid JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER set' at import time (comment at run_differs.py:397-398)
Code: test_decision_census.py:36 `return _load('run_differs_census_test', 'run_differs.py')` — standalone spec_from_file_location load with no rebar.toml in scope
Code: run_differs._load() uses sys.modules pre-registration as the injection seam (lazy_load pattern) — not select_backend()
Plan: 'introduce a new seam (a _load_acli-equivalent or a backend-transport injection point)' — vague on which mechanism, doesn't address the config-read dependency
findings.finding: The plan proposes that `run_differs.py:403` gets a new seam routed through `select_backend(config).transport`, but `run_differs` is loaded standalone via `spec_from_file_location` in multiple test files (confirmed: `test_decision_census.py:36`, `test_recovery_loud_failures.py:33`, `test_orchestrator_wiring.py:506`). `select_backend(config)` requires calling `load_config()`, which reads `rebar.toml` — a file absent in most isolated test tmp dirs. The current workaround (`from rebar_reconciler import acli as acli_mod_for_comments` inside the function body) defers the import safely. The plan says 'add a small _load_acli-equivalent seam' but doesn't specify whether it uses the same sys.modules pre-registration pattern that the existing `_load()` helper in run_differs uses, nor how it avoids triggering `load_config()` during standalone-loaded test execution.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — 'run_differs.py:403' bullet; Approach section — '(2) run_differs.py:403 and _attestation.py:82' paragraph
scenarios: New seam calls `select_backend(load_config())` at the top of `_run_differs_outbound`; standalone-loaded test that calls `_run_differs_binding_walk` or any path touching this function fails with ConfigError or FileNotFoundError on missing rebar.toml
Implementer wraps the seam in a try/except to handle config absence, silently masking real backend-selection errors in production

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which Backend/IdentityConvention attribute or method supplies the `"jira"` string values needed by `validate_creation_channel(...)` and `ensure_identity_for(provider, ...)` in `apply_inbound_records.py`, or explicitly note that these two literals are EXEMPT from replacement (as they are closed-vocabulary values tied to the reducer schema, not just Jira-module imports).
evidence: Code: apply_inbound_records.py:156 `create_data['creation_channel'] = validate_creation_channel('jira')` — closed-vocabulary reducer event field
Code: apply_inbound_records.py:70-74 `rebar.ensure_identity_for('jira', account_id, ...)` — vendor provider string
S2 ticket (ce4d): IdentityConvention 'expressed over RemoteRef' — governs rebar-id label read/write, not creation_channel or ensure_identity_for provider strings
ABSENCE: Neither the S2 Backend Protocol spec nor this plan specifies a Backend/IdentityConvention property that yields the `"jira"` string needed by validate_creation_channel or ensure_identity_for
findings.finding: The plan says to replace the `apply_inbound_records.py` `"jira"` literals with `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention`, but one of those literals (`validate_creation_channel("jira")` at line 156) is a CLOSED-VOCABULARY value written to the event store — not a provider selector. `IdentityConvention` (as defined in S2/ce4d) governs the `rebar-id` label stamp/read convention; it is not specified to expose a `creation_channel` string or `provider_key` property. Similarly, `ensure_identity_for("jira", ...)` at line 70 passes `"jira"` as a vendor-provider literal whose replacement path through the Backend port is unspecified. Without specifying what Backend method or attribute provides these string values (e.g. `backend.identity_convention.creation_channel` or `backend.provider_key`), an implementer has no clear substitution target.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Replace the apply_inbound_records.py "jira" literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention'; AC bullet 3
scenarios: Implementer replaces `validate_creation_channel("jira")` with `backend.identity_convention.vendor` (guessing the attribute name) → either AttributeError at runtime or wrong string value written to event store, corrupting reducer provenance
Implementer leaves the ensure_identity_for("jira", ...) call unchanged because there's no clear Backend method to replace it → the AC 'no hardcoded "jira" literal' grep fails

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `inbound_fields.py` is a Jira-concrete ('vendor') module subject to the same rewire as `outbound_fields`/`adf`; if yes, add `inbound_fields` to the neutrality grep ban list in AC#1 and confirm it is covered by the 'root vendor modules' phrase; if no, remove `inbound_differ.py:141` from the confirmed leak sites list.
evidence: Plan: 'inbound_differ.py:141 — _map_jira_to_local_fields(…) (inbound mapper)'
Code search shows: `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_fields import … _map_jira_to_local_fields` at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_differ.py:31`
`inbound_fields.py` resides at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` — same directory as core modules, NOT under `adapters/jira/`
AC #1 neutrality grep bans `rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.*` and `outbound_fields`/`adf`/`comment_limits`/`acli*` — `inbound_fields` is absent from the ban list
findings.finding: The plan classifies `inbound_differ.py:141` as importing from a 'vendor mapper' (Jira-specific module), but `_map_jira_to_local_fields` is actually imported from `rebar_reconciler.inbound_fields` — a core sibling file at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py`, not from `adapters/jira/*`. The neutrality gate AC (#1) only enumerates `adapters.jira.*` and `outbound_fields`/`adf`/`comment_limits`/`acli*` as banned imports; `inbound_fields` is absent from that list. If `inbound_fields.py` is also a Jira-concrete module that the plan intends to route through the Backend port, the scope and neutrality grep need to cover it explicitly; if not, `inbound_differ.py` is not an actual leak site by the plan's own criteria.
findings.location: Context / Problem — 'Confirmed leak sites', `inbound_differ.py:141`; Acceptance Criteria AC#1
scenarios: Executor rewires inbound_differ.py to call backend.inbound_mapper() but the neutrality grep passes because the grep doesn't cover inbound_fields — the leak continues undetected.
Executor finds inbound_differ.py already passes the neutrality gate (inbound_fields not banned) and skips it, but the plan intended it to be rewired — scope ambiguity causes a missed rewire.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `inbound_fields` to the neutrality gate's banned-import list alongside `outbound_fields`/`adf`/`comment_limits`/`acli*`, and add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` to the modules whose logic should be single-sourced behind the adapter (it contains the Jira-to-local status/type/priority maps, which are as Jira-specific as the outbound maps).

findings.finding: `apply_handlers.py:181` is an unenumerated consumer of the `_load_acli` seam contract change. It catches `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError`, where `ctx.acli` is the value returned by `applier._load_acli()` threaded through `BatchApplyContext`. The plan evolves `applier._load_acli` to 'yield the backend transport' but never names `apply_handlers.py` as an edit target, even though changing the shape of what `_load_acli` returns directly changes what `ctx.acli` is and whether `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` resolves.
findings.location: Approach section (applier/fetcher _load_acli seam evolution); apply_handlers.py:69,181; applier.py:654-656

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the Backend facade delivered by S2 (ce4d) exposes a `.transport` attribute (not a factory method or differently-named field) matching what the Approach and AC describe, or update the attribute reference to match the actual S2 interface spec.
evidence: grep for 'class Backend' in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler returns no matches
grep for '_backend_registry' returns no matches
grep for 'TicketTransport' returns no matches
grep for 'select_backend' in src/ returns no matches
S2 ticket (ce4d) shows _backend.py is listed as 'dep absent' in the plan-review manifest — confirming it does not yet exist at the plan-review time
S3 ticket (94f9) shows _backend_registry.py is listed as 'dep absent' — confirming it does not yet exist
findings.finding: The plan's Approach section asserts that `run_differs.py:403` will be routed through `select_backend(config).transport` — treating `.transport` as a known attribute name on the Backend facade. However, `_backend.py` (S2) and `_backend_registry.py` (S3) do not yet exist in the repo, so the attribute name `.transport` on the Backend/JiraBackend is unverified codebase fact. If S2 names the transport capability differently (e.g. `.ticket_transport`, `.create_client()`, or wraps it as a `TicketTransport` Protocol object rather than a named `.transport` field), the routing described here won't compile.
findings.location: Approach section: 'route it directly through select_backend(config).transport (the TicketTransport)'; also AC line 4: 'routed through select_backend(config).transport'
scenarios: S2 lands and names the transport accessor differently than `.transport` — executor writes `select_backend(config).transport` and gets AttributeError at runtime.

S2 wraps transport construction differently (e.g. `backend.make_transport(settings)` rather than a pre-constructed `.transport`) — the `run_differs.py` routing logic described in the Approach doesn't match the actual interface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the run_differs.py seam design fork: state which shape to use (e.g., 'add a `_load_acli`-equivalent function that returns `select_backend(config).transport`, matching the applier/fetcher seam pattern') so the executor has a single unambiguous implementation target.
evidence: 'introduce a new seam (a `_load_acli`-equivalent or a backend-transport injection point) as an explicit prerequisite step'
The plan is otherwise highly specific (naming exact line numbers and seam shapes for applier/fetcher), making this 'or' conspicuous as an unresolved design choice.
findings.finding: The run_differs.py seam design is stated as 'a `_load_acli`-equivalent OR a backend-transport injection point', leaving a real architectural fork unresolved for the executor — the two options have different implications for how the new seam is patched in tests and how S5 (physical relocation) will interact with it.
findings.location: Approach section, run_differs.py paragraph: 'introduce a new seam (a `_load_acli`-equivalent or a backend-transport injection point)'
scenarios: An agent picks option A (`_load_acli`-equivalent); a reviewer expected option B (backend-transport injection); the test structure diverges from future S5 assumptions.
The two options produce different patch targets in tests — choosing wrong means the new test doesn't match the seam shape that S5 will refactor.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'For run_differs.py, introduce a `_load_acli`-equivalent private function (e.g. `_load_transport`) that calls `select_backend(config).transport` and is patched via `patch.object(run_differs, "_load_transport", ...)` in the new test — matching the applier/fetcher seam shape for consistency with S5.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC4 to explicitly include _attestation.py: add a clause such as 'and _attestation.py routes build_acli_client_from_env through select_backend(config).transport, verified by an updated or new test covering that construction path.'
evidence: Context: '_attestation.py:82 — via build_acli_client_from_env (_attestation.py:41): route through the backend transport; update its construction path.'
Approach: 'For _attestation, route build_acli_client_from_env through the backend transport likewise.'

AC4: 'The existing applier/fetcher _load_acli seams and their 23 patch.object tests pass... and run_differs.py:403... gets a new seam... all verified by make test.' — _attestation.py is not mentioned.
AC1 only verifies absence of direct vendor imports/AcliClient construction, not that _attestation correctly routes through the backend transport.
findings.finding: The _attestation.py transport-routing is described as a deliverable in Context and Approach ('route build_acli_client_from_env through the backend transport likewise') but AC4 — the only criterion that verifies transport-site changes — names only applier/fetcher and run_differs.py, leaving _attestation's positive behavioral requirement (correct routing through backend transport) uncovered by any acceptance criterion.
findings.location: AC4 vs. Context ('_attestation.py:82') and Approach ('For _attestation, route build_acli_client_from_env through the backend transport likewise')
scenarios: _attestation routes through backend transport but has a bug in the construction path — AC1 passes (no direct AcliClient import) but no criterion catches the incorrect routing
The _attestation change is accidentally omitted and no AC fails explicitly

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test-modification callout for `_attestation.py` in AC4 and the Testing section — either update its existing tests to patch the new backend-transport construction path, or add a new unit test that verifies `build_acli_client_from_env` delegates to `select_backend(config).transport` after the rewire.
evidence: AC4 lists applier/fetcher seam tests and a new run_differs test but does not mention _attestation.
The Approach section says 'route `build_acli_client_from_env` through the backend transport' with no corresponding test update instruction.
ABSENCE: no 'update _attestation tests' or 'add _attestation test' item in Testing, Scope, or AC sections.
findings.finding: `_attestation.py:82` is one of the 4 named transport construction sites whose construction path changes (`build_acli_client_from_env` is re-routed through the backend transport), but the plan names no test modification or new test for this changed path — only applier/fetcher (23 existing seam tests) and run_differs (1 new test) get explicit test callouts.
findings.location: AC4; Approach section — '_attestation.py:82 — via build_acli_client_from_env … route through the backend transport; update its construction path'
scenarios: The _attestation transport path is rewired but existing attestation tests still mock the old `build_acli_client_from_env` path; the rewired path is untested, so a wiring bug passes silently.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, append: 'and `_attestation.py`'s `build_acli_client_from_env` is covered by an updated or new unit test that patches the backend-transport path and asserts correct credential forwarding.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that evolving the `_load_acli` seam to return a `TicketTransport` (instead of the acli module) is test-compatible and clean, but does not address `apply_handlers.py:181`'s `except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError as exc:` — an exception-class attribute lookup on `ctx.acli` that will break when the seam stops returning the module. After the rewire, `ctx.acli` is no longer the acli module and does not expose `AssigneeNotFoundError`, so this `except` clause will either raise `AttributeError` at runtime or silently fail to catch the exception, allowing `AssigneeNotFoundError` to propagate as an unhandled error.
findings.location: Approach section ('evolve the seam + its `patch.object` tests') + apply_handlers.py:181 (`except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError as exc`)
findings.suggested_fix: Define `AssigneeNotFoundError` as a first-class exception on the `Backend` protocol (or its `TicketTransport`) so `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` becomes `ctx.backend.transport.AssigneeNotFoundError` (or `ctx.backend.AssigneeNotFoundError`). Update `apply_handlers.py:181` and `outbound_assignee.py:113` in lockstep. Add a dedicated test that injects a transport mock carrying the exception class and confirms the soft-fail path fires.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile Context's 'not where files live' with Scope's 'Relocate into adapters/jira/' by clarifying that 'Relocate' means moving the logic/code into the adapter's *existing* files (not a new file or filesystem move) — or replace 'Relocate' with 'Consolidate/inline' and add a parenthetical matching AC2's 'path-agnostic' footnote.
evidence: Context: 'It changes who calls what, not where files live (physical relocation is S5)'
Scope: 'Relocate ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link-constant duplicate into adapters/jira/ (single-sourced)'
AC2 partially resolves this with '(path-agnostic — the file move itself is S5)' but the Scope still uses the verb 'Relocate'
findings.finding: The Context section states 'It changes who calls what, not where files live (physical relocation is S5)' but the Scope section says 'Relocate ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link-constant duplicate into adapters/jira/' — an implementer reading both sections receives contradictory instructions about whether logic physically moves in this story.
findings.location: Context paragraph 2 vs. Scope bullet 2
scenarios: Implementer treats 'Relocate into adapters/jira/' as moving files, violating the 'not where files live' constraint and colliding with S5 scope
Implementer treats 'not where files live' as the rule and skips the logic consolidation, failing AC2's single-sourcing check
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Relocate ADF-sizing...' in Scope with 'Consolidate ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link-constant duplicate into the existing adapters/jira/ mapper code (single-sourced; file-system moves are S5)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out statement for the _load_acli seam contract change: either (a) describe an expand-contract sequence (add new seam, migrate callers, delete old seam) that keeps tests green at each step, or (b) state that the entire seam migration is a single atomic commit and document the revert command/procedure (e.g., 'revert the single commit; no partial states are valid').
evidence: Approach: 'evolve the seam + its patch.object tests in the same change (23 test files use that seam — they inject types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client))'
Approach: 'the seam keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy' — contract evolution detail is vague
No rollback path, revert strategy, or expand-contract sequence is described for the seam change affecting 23 test files
T4 requires: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
findings.finding: The plan changes the `_load_acli` seam contract for applier and fetcher (seam now yields a backend transport instead of a SimpleNamespace/AcliClient-providing object), acknowledges that 23 test files must be updated atomically, but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan if the atomic cut-over is only partially applied or needs to be reverted.
findings.location: Approach section — '_load_acli seam' paragraphs; AC4
scenarios: Story is merged in a half-applied state (some seam callers updated, some not) — 23 test patches now target a seam with a changed return type and silently pass or fail unpredictably
A production incident requires reverting to pre-story main — no documented revert procedure exists for the seam migration
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'The _load_acli seam change is delivered as a single atomic commit; to revert, git revert that commit restores all 23 test patches and both seam callers to their pre-story state. No partial application is valid — CI enforces this via the neutrality gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR/AGENTS.md update to Scope and AC that (a) corrects the stale §(a) leak-site list (remove `baseline_shadow.py`, add `apply_inbound.py`) and (b) documents the Backend-port routing convention, the `run_differs` seam pattern, and the `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` replacing provider literals.
evidence: 'the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names `baseline_shadow.py`, which does not exist, and omits `apply_inbound.py`'
Scope section lists 4 bullet points; none mentions updating the ADR or AGENTS.md
No AC line requires an ADR/design-doc update
New patterns introduced: Backend port as sole routing layer, `run_differs` seam, `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` convention — all architectural decisions
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges the existing ADR §(a) list is stale (references non-existent `baseline_shadow.py`, omits `apply_inbound.py`) but does not include an ADR/design-doc update in its Scope or Acceptance Criteria. Additionally, this story introduces novel patterns — Backend-port-as-mandatory-routing-layer for all core consumers, the new `run_differs`-specific seam, and `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` replacing provider literals — that constitute architectural decisions requiring documentation capture.
findings.location: Context section ('the ADR §(a) list is stale'); Scope section (no ADR update listed); AC list (no ADR update AC)
scenarios: A future developer consults the ADR to enumerate leak sites and is misled by the stale `baseline_shadow.py` reference or misses `apply_inbound.py`
A developer adding a new core module doesn't know the Backend-port routing convention exists, re-introduces a direct vendor import, and the pattern drifts
The `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` convention is undocumented, so a subsequent story uses a raw string literal again
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fifth Scope bullet: 'Update the ADR §(a) leak-site list and document the Backend-port routing convention, `run_differs` seam pattern, and `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` in AGENTS.md or the ADR.' Add a corresponding AC: '[ ] ADR §(a) list matches the actual rewired modules; AGENTS.md (or ADR) names the Backend-port routing convention and `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` rule — verified by review.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling and at least one observable signal (log.error / metric) for the new `run_differs.py:403` seam when `select_backend(config).transport` fails or `select_backend` raises, so operators can distinguish a backend-misconfiguration failure from a Jira connectivity failure.
evidence: 'run_differs.py:403 — no _load_acli seam exists here. It does a bare from rebar_reconciler import acli as acli_mod_for_comments'
'introduce a new seam (a _load_acli-equivalent or a backend-transport injection point) as an explicit prerequisite step, route the outbound-diff client through select_backend(config).transport'
Plan specifies the new seam's test coverage but says nothing about what happens when select_backend() itself raises or returns a transport that cannot be constructed
ABSENCE: no mention of try/except, logging, fallback, or degraded-mode for this new call path
findings.finding: The new `run_differs.py:403` seam — the only site with no pre-existing `_load_acli` seam — is routed through `select_backend(config).transport` with no specified error handling if backend selection fails, if the transport is misconfigured, or if `select_backend` raises (e.g., no backend registered, missing config key). The plan is silent on what the caller observes and whether the failure is logged/surfaced to an operator. This is a newly-introduced failure point (unlike applier/fetcher which evolve existing seams), so the gap is unambiguous.
findings.location: Approach section — `run_differs.py:403` new seam; Acceptance Criteria AC-4
scenarios: Backend registry is empty or misconfigured at runtime — select_backend() raises; run_differs swallows or propagates with an opaque traceback and no operator-visible log
Transport construction from the selected backend fails due to missing env vars — silent failure vs. the old direct AcliClient() which would have raised a clear AttributeError at the same line
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `select_backend(config).transport` call in a try/except that logs the exception with context (module, config key attempted) and re-raises so the caller's existing error boundary is preserved; add one log.warning at the new seam's construction site mirroring the log already present at the applier/fetcher seams (if any).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the title to say 'single-source' or 'consolidate' rather than 'relocate' (e.g. 'Route all core consumers through the Backend port; single-source ADF-sizing/lossy-status/maps into adapters/jira logic without physical file moves (deferred to S5)') so the title and body agree on the scope boundary.
evidence: Title: 'relocate ADF-sizing/lossy-status/maps into adapters/jira'
Context: 'It changes who calls what, not where files live (physical relocation is S5)'
AC2: 'assertion is core-emptiness + single-sourcing, path-agnostic — the file move itself is S5'
findings.finding: The plan title says 'relocate ADF-sizing/lossy-status/maps into adapters/jira' but the body clarifies the file move is deferred to S5 — only the logic consolidation/single-sourcing happens here. The word 'relocate' in the title implies physical file movement, which contradicts the body's explicit 'path-agnostic' AC2 and the 'not where files live' framing in Context. A developer reading only the title will expect file moves that S5 actually does, creating confusion about story boundaries.
findings.location: Plan title and AC2: 'assertion is core-emptiness + single-sourcing, path-agnostic — the file move itself is S5'
scenarios: A developer scoping this story from the title expects to physically move adf.py, outbound_fields.py, comment_limits.py etc. into adapters/jira/ as part of this ticket, then discovers S5 also moves those same files — either duplicating work or creating a merge conflict between S4 and S5 scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'relocate' in the title to 'single-source' and add a parenthetical '(file moves deferred to S5)' to match the body's explicit framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'compare to pre-story `main` output' oracle with an independently-authored, domain-verified fixture set (expected reconcile events authored from spec, not captured from a running build), so the characterization suite detects both new regressions AND pre-existing defects rather than encoding them.
evidence: "the full reconcile suite (outbound + inbound passes over fixture stores) produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story `main` — the primary behavior-preserving guard"
Pre-story `main` output is used as the oracle, not an independently-authored or domain-verified fixture set.
findings.finding: The primary behavior-preserving guard compares reconcile output to 'pre-story `main`' — this is the snapshot-of-current-output / change-detector anti-pattern: if `main` already has a bug in a reconcile path, the characterization suite pins that bug as the oracle.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Characterization: the full reconcile suite … produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story `main`'
scenarios: If current main has a latent reconcile bug (e.g., wrong ADF truncation boundary), the characterization test encodes that bug as correct, and the story closes green while the defect is preserved in the rewired code.
findings.suggested_fix: Author a small set of fixture reconcile scenarios (2–3 outbound + 2–3 inbound) where the expected output events/field values are written by hand from the domain spec (e.g., 'field X maps to Jira field Y with value Z per the mapping table'), not captured by running the current code. Use those as the oracle. Keep the full fixture-store run as a smoke/non-regression check but do not let it be the sole behavioral guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one live/end-to-end acceptance criterion (or explicitly name a child ticket that carries it) that exercises the `select_backend(config).transport` path against a real or realistic Jira boundary (e.g., a contract/integration test that constructs an actual `AcliClient` via the new backend route and confirms the transport is correctly initialized with credentials), so the cutover cannot close green with a broken live path.
evidence: "23 test files use that seam — they inject `types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)`" — these bypass the new backend transport entirely.
The new run_differs seam test 'patches the new seam' — also an offline mock.
No live/integration criterion for the transport cutover is named; the plan explicitly defers physical file moves to S5 but does not defer live-transport validation to any named child ticket.
The proxy-validation ANTI-FP exemptions do not apply: the transport path has a real external boundary (Jira API via AcliClient), and no named child ticket defers live exercise.
findings.finding: The Backend-port cutover makes `select_backend(config).transport` the new default for all 4 transport construction sites, but every test path exercises this through mocks/fakes/fixture stores — no live/end-to-end acceptance criterion is specified, so the suite can go green while the new default path is broken against a real Jira boundary.
findings.location: Testing section — transport seam tests; AC4 ('all verified by `make test`'); Approach — '_load_acli' seam evolution with 'types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)'
scenarios: The Backend port's `select_backend(config).transport` wiring could silently produce a None/wrong transport, fail to pass credentials, or omit required headers; all mock-based tests pass because the mock satisfies the seam contract without exercising the real construction path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or a named follow-on ticket (e.g., S4-live-smoke) requiring a single integration test that calls `select_backend(config).transport` with a test-environment Jira config and asserts the resulting transport object is a real `AcliClient` with the expected URL/credentials — not a mock. This exercises the construction path the mocks bypass.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
